I noticed the cemetery has several stones with only Hebrew initials—could any of them belong to families who left so abruptly that no one had time to carve full names, or were those perhaps markers for people whose identities were deliberately erased over time?

When you’ve been clearing brush near the eastern edge of the cemetery for decades, have you ever found fragments of ritual objects—tallit pins, broken menorahs, or even small stones left on graves—that made you feel like you were touching something sacred no one else remembered?

Mrs. Whitmore mentioned “hidden graves” in passing—do you think those are literal unmarked plots, or could they be metaphors for families who converted, fled, or were forgotten because they no longer identified as Jewish?

If the last Jewish family in town left in 1972, why did the synagogue’s roof collapse so slowly—was it neglect, or did the community hold on to it longer than anyone admitted, hoping someone would return?

Have you ever felt like you were being watched while working in the cemetery—not by people, but by something older, like the weight of prayers that were never finished, or names that were never spoken aloud again?

The city records say the cemetery was deeded to the Methodist church after the Jewish congregation dissolved—was that a legal transfer, or did it feel more like a quiet theft, and did Mrs. Whitmore ever question why no Jewish voice objected at the time?

When you hear the wind rustle through the cedars near the oldest stones, do you ever imagine what songs or lullabies the women who buried their children there might have hummed, and do you think those melodies still linger?

Have you ever wondered if the soil here, soaked in generations of tears and prayers, remembers more than we can read on cracked stone—and if so, what would it say if it could speak in a language we still understand?

When you transcribe these recordings later, how will you decide which silences to preserve—the pauses where Mrs. Whitmore looks away, or the moments when she stops speaking because the memory is too heavy to name?
Do you think the children who once ran through this cemetery before the 1950s still dream of it—and if they do, do they dream of it as a place of joy, or of endings they never understood?

The Mishnah in Mo’ed Katan speaks of burying the dead before sundown—yet here in Eufaula, the cemetery is half-swallowed by kudzu and time. How did the community reconcile the urgency of Jewish burial law with the slow, quiet erosion of their presence in this Southern town?

I’ve traced the names on these weathered stones, but the ledger only gives me one name from 1938. Could there have been others who left quietly, without memorial, whose stories were lost to migration, assimilation, or shame?

The candle flickers in the window of my study—outside, the Alabama night hums with cicadas. Do the spirits of those buried in Eufaula still hear the call of the shofar on Rosh Hashanah, even when no one is left to blow it?

Tractate Mo’ed Katan says the land where a Jew is buried becomes holy ground—but who decides when that holiness is no longer honored? Is it the last mourner who visits, or the last stone left standing?

I found a faded photograph of the B’nai Israel synagogue from 1923—there’s a woman in the back row holding a prayer book, her face half-obscured. Could she have been the one who last lit the ner tamid? And if so, where did she go?

When I study these laws of kavod hamet, I feel the weight of responsibility. But who is left to hold me accountable? The dead? The silence? Or the ghosts of congregants who once whispered prayers in Yiddish beneath these same pines?
